segments.title: British sheds and wartime invention
segments.summary: A live discussion frames British shed invention as a mix of private tinkering, military workshops, and wartime engineering culture.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens the episode by defining a British shed broadly enough to include shore workshops, home experimentation, and the informal spaces where engineers keep working after official hours. He cautions that this makes the ranking more land- and air-focused than a normal naval episode, while still connecting it to Royal Navy workshop culture and the problem of how useful inventions move from personal tinkering into service use. The early jet-engine discussion then illustrates the broader theme: wartime systems rarely come from one tidy inventor story, but from overlapping technical papers, official programs, private effort, and disrupted international careers.

evidence.note: The opening defines the shed premise, explains why many naval inventions happened in shipboard workshops rather than literal sheds, and frames the episode as more land- and air-focused.
Dr. Clarke compares the shed to a design workspace for engineers and scientists, then describes people who go home and continue tinkering because they do not switch off.
The first major example is the jet engine, tied to Frank Whittle, wider technical theory, a Spanish naval air engineer's plans, and the interwar push for faster aircraft.
